title: Hook knife technique
subtitle: How to hold, grip, and cut with the hook knife for spoon-carving
excerpt: The hook knife is the tool that hollows a spoon bowl. This tutorial covers grip, cut direction, orientation, and the specific movements that produce a clean hollow without chatter or tearing.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the pull-blade shaping method; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for the curved-tool grip conventions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A curved-bladed knife for hollowing the bowl of a spoon or kuksa. Available in small, medium, large, and deep-hook radii.
A cut where the blade moves toward the body. Used only with the workpiece braced against the chest so the blade-arc cannot reach anything vital.
A leather strap charged with abrasive compound, used to maintain a sharp edge between sharpenings.

content.text: The
hook knife

does one thing: it hollows the inside of a spoon bowl or kuksa cup. Everything about the grip, the stroke, and the body position is aimed at that one movement cleanly and safely. A beginner who has only used a sloyd knife will find the hook knife different in every way except the sharpening cadence.
When to use the hook knife

Use the hook knife after the back of the spoon bowl has been domed with the sloyd knife and before the final surface refinement. The hook knife removes the bulk of the bowl hollow. A No. 7 sweep gouge can do the same job on seasoned wood; for green-wood spoon carving the hook knife is the standard because it works with minimal force and leaves a clean surface that needs little further work.
What you need

A hook knife, medium radius is the all-rounder. The small radius is for tasting spoons and tight kuksa interiors; the large radius is for ladle bowls. The wood should be green: seasoned hard wood resists the hook knife, and the cuts required are longer and harder to control. The edge must be sharp enough to shave hair from the back of the forearm before you start; a dull hook knife drags and chatters and forces you to push harder, which destabilises the grip.
Orientation and grip

Hold the spoon blank bowl-face-up in your non-knife hand. The handle of the spoon points away from your body; the bowl is between your hands. The hook knife is held in your dominant hand with the handle in a closed fist, the hook facing toward you. The first grip is the pull grip: thumb on the back of the knife handle, four fingers wrapped around the front, cutting edge facing toward the body and up.
This is a
pull cut

configuration. The blade travels toward your body. The safety condition that makes this acceptable is that the workpiece (the spoon blank) is held between your knife hand and your body, and the blade-arc stays inside that space. The knife hand does not leave the workpiece; the forearm draws the blade through the wood, not the shoulder. The movement is small: 30 to 40 mm of blade travel per stroke.
The chest-brace alternative

An alternative grip puts the spoon blank against the chest, bowl facing up and away from your body. The knife hand moves in the same pull direction but the arc is below the chest rather than across it. Some carvers find this easier for the first few cuts because the body braces the blank from behind. The risk is the same: if the blade slips, it moves toward you. Keep cuts short and controlled.
The cut stroke, step by step
Position the hook at the far rim of the bowl, edge in contact with the wood, at about 45 degrees to the bowl surface.
Draw the knife toward you in an arc, using the forearm only. The hook travels from the rim toward the centre of the bowl.
Stop the cut when the blade has reached the bowl centre. Do not continue past the centre toward the near rim; lifting the blade at the centre keeps the hollow even.
Rotate the blank slightly, reposition the hook at the far rim, and repeat the arc from a new angle. Working in overlapping arcs around the bowl produces an even hollow.
After three or four passes around the bowl, reverse the blank 180 degrees and repeat from the other side. This ensures the hollow is symmetrical.
What a successful cut looks like

A clean hook-knife cut lifts a thin curved shaving that curls away on its own. The cut surface is smooth with slight tool facets; no tearing or raised fibres. The shaving is continuous along the arc from rim to centre. If the shaving breaks into short segments, the edge is dull: strop and try again. If the surface looks torn, you are cutting across a grain reversal; rotate the blank 90 degrees and take a light test cut to find the direction that cuts cleanly.
The strop cadence
The
strop

is used every ten to fifteen minutes of hook-knife work. A hook knife dulls faster than a sloyd knife because the cutting arc is tighter and the edge load is higher. To strop the inside of the hook, lay the leather flat on a hard surface and draw the inside radius across it with the edge trailing. Three to five passes per side restores the working edge.
Common mistakes
Taking too long a stroke: pulling the blade past the bowl centre and into the near rim creates a lopsided hollow. Stop at the centre each time.
Gripping the blank too tightly: a tense grip in the blank hand transmits movement to the bowl and the cut wanders. Hold the blank firmly but not clenched.

Working on seasoned wood: the hook knife is a green-wood tool. Seasoned hardwood requires a gouge and mallet to hollow a bowl safely; the forces on the hook knife in seasoned wood are too high for controlled one-handed cuts.
Not checking sharpness before starting: a dull hook knife forces you to push harder, which breaks the control of the cut. Check and strop before the first stroke.
Using the right-handed hook on a left-handed blank: hook knives come in left- and right-handed versions. The orientation matters; the wrong hand will produce awkward cut angles and poor control.
